turns.content: I'm trying to understand how the macro system works in Rust, but I keep thinking macros are just pre-processor directives like C++. It seems like everything is compiled before runtime. How does something like the inventory crate actually register plugins at runtime if there's no central registry?

That's a common misconception for beginners. In Rust, macros aren't run-time instructions like in Python or JavaScript; they are compile-time transformations that expand into regular code. The inventory crate uses a special macro called collect! which triggers during compilation to gather all the plugin definitions from various crates into a single list stored in an InventoryRegistry struct. This allows you to add new plugins without changing the core code, but it's still done at compile time.
So I can't just call a function on the registry later? That sounds restrictive for my use case where I need dynamic loading. Is there a way to make this truly runtime based like some other systems?

You're right that standard Rust macros don't run at execution time, but you can achieve similar effects using traits and generics combined with const evaluation or procedural macros. For the inventory crate specifically, they use a trait called PluginRegistry that defines how plugins are collected, and then implement this trait for your specific plugin types so the compiler knows what to include. You could also explore crates like proc-macro2 if you need more complex runtime logic.
Hey, I'm working on a Rust project and I need to register plugins dynamically at runtime. How can I do that without using a central registry?

Great question! For distributed plugin registration without a central registry, you can use the `inventory` crate in combination with macros and procedural macros. It’s idiomatic and keeps your code clean.
I’m thinking of just creating a trait for plugins and then using some macro to collect them at runtime. Is that right?
Yes, that's correct! You define a `Plugin` trait, then use the `inventory::collect!` macro to gather all implementations dynamically. This is very idiomatic Rust.
Okay, so I’ll have an inventory like this: `inventory!(pub static PLUGINS: Plugin = ...);`. Then how do I collect them at runtime?
Right! You can use the `inventory::iter` function to iterate over all collected plugins. It’s very much a Rustic way of doing dynamic plugin loading.
What if I forget to call `collect!` before iterating? Will that cause problems?
Good question! If you forget, the iterator will be empty, so no plugins will load. So just remember to put a call to `inventory::collect!(MyPlugin);` in your main function or initialization code.
That makes sense. Thanks for the clarification! I’ll go ahead and implement it then.
You're welcome! If you run into any issues, feel free to ask. Happy coding! 🚀

I'm trying to understand how the inventory crate handles plugin registration, but it feels like a lot of boilerplate compared to just using a central registry. It reminds me of how Rust's standard library uses derive macros instead of manual trait implementations for common patterns.

The inventory crate actually avoids the single point of failure that a central registry creates by letting each plugin define its own entry points. Instead of one global `collect!` macro managing everything, every plugin registers itself locally using the `register_plugin` helper or direct trait implementations like `PluginProvider`. This distributes the registration logic so no single component can crash the whole system if it fails.
So I don't need to worry about a global registry? But then how does the host application actually discover which plugins are loaded without some kind of central coordination?

The host application doesn't rely on a single discovery mechanism; it relies on the fact that every plugin implements the same trait, like `PluginProvider` or `InventoryItemProvider`. When you iterate over all loaded plugins at runtime using `for plugin in plugins.iter()`, each one naturally contributes its items to the shared context. The distributed nature means no central registry is needed—the collection happens implicitly through the trait implementations.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how a macro like `collect!` fits in if there's no central registry. Is it just a regular function call?

The `collect!` macro here isn't doing something magical beyond what you'd expect from a standard iterator collection, but its power comes from how plugins are structured. Each plugin defines items using the same trait, so when you call `inventory.collect()`, it simply iterates through all registered instances and merges their data into one cohesive inventory. The real magic is in how the crate ensures these implementations are consistent across different plugins.
I see, but what if I want to register a new plugin? Does it require specific macros or just implementing traits?

You can implement the necessary traits directly without needing special macros for registration. The crate provides helper functions like `register_plugin` that you call from your main function, but the core logic lives in your trait implementations. This flexibility means you can mix and match different approaches depending on your plugin's complexity.
Okay, so I just need to make sure my plugin implements the right traits and call `register_plugin`. But what about error handling if a plugin fails to start?

Since there's no central registry, errors from individual plugins are isolated. Your host application can check each plugin's status independently using the trait's methods or by examining return values during initialization. This way, a failed registration in one plugin doesn't prevent others from loading.
